transcript.transcript: Just a little bit of Don's extracurricular activities here in the city of Coeur d'Alene. In interviewing Don, it's kind of hard to get him to talk about himself but he's been involved in a great number of worthwhile projects within the city for the city not only locally but statewide and I might just ask Don a few things, to comment on a few things that he was involved in. Most people who have lived in in Coeur d'Alene a period of time know that that Park and Recreation Department really holds, takes a backseat to no one and we realized that for a number of years that we had an outstanding Park and Recreation Director in Red Halpern. We also are astute enough to know that no matter how good a director is, sometimes you need some good direction and he needs some backing. One of the things that Don Johnson was on was he was on the Park and Recreation Committee and he was not only on it as committee member, he was a chairman of it for a period of time. So Don, maybe if you could just elaborate a little bit from your recollection of where it started and really to what it's involved in today. Well Denny, as far as Parks and Recreation, I got to go back to when I was in grade school and Jim Egan, when my involvement with the Parks and Recreation is that if somebody had a baseball, Jim Egan ran out of the basement of the old Central School and somebody bring a ball and somebody bring a bat and Jim would help organize a softball game or a baseball game and and that was really the Park and Recreation Department. But then I went on to school and by the time I got back in 1953, the city I believe had hired Red and things had certainly turned around because Red is a professional recreation director. And when I got on the Parks and Recreation Commission, it was still rather small, but Red was in real control of the situation. But things were rather loose. I can remember the dance that was being held in the park. Red would go down at the end and collect the receipts and throw them in the jockey box of the car and go to the bank the next morning. And those kind of things bothered us so we in that respect we helped Red get the act together there but but Red was so involved that never thought a thing about those type of things but he received criticism for him and I think that's an area we helped him get a little more sophistication in. But Red was the ultimate park and recreation man. He was out, as we know now, with involvement on higher levels, state levels, and even world levels. Through that, he was continually educating himself to recreational needs and feeding them back into the community. He was constantly looking for new programs to put together. I look at the girls' softball program as starting right with Red Halpern, even at the local level and in the national level, because Red has always been totally involved in softball and to me he has opened up a incredible world of athletes to the female section of our community and that's in the softball area. It's grown from practically one small sandlot team into something 70, 80 teams at this time and it's an incredible program and it's just one example of I think how fortunate we are to have a person of rich caliber here in the city of Coeur d'Alene. And I think we have one of the finest recreation programs in the state. Okay, that's great. Another thing, Don, that I'd like you to comment on, another one of your activities that you were involved in, you were the president of the Association of Idaho Cities the period of 1981 through 1982. I guess most of us would, by chance, just say this is an association of different Idaho cities. Why did you feel it important enough to get involved with that in the respect that, how did you feel that that would directly help the city of Coeur d'Alene? Well, when I first went to Boise to my first association meeting, I had a little bit of a chip on my shoulder wondering, you know, why are we involved with the city of Coeur d'Alene? And I'll have to admit after my first meeting and sitting there visiting with other councilmen and mayors from throughout the state and the exchange of ideas and the exchange of different ways to approach problems was very helpful. And each meeting from that point on that I attended enabled me to enlarge and to bring back from Boise and from the other mayors and councilmen throughout the state ideas and how to better solve the problems here in Coeur d'Alene. And the exchange of those ideas I think was very fruitful. Plus the other thing through the years the legislature from time to time forgets that there is local government in Idaho. And that's not by design, it's just the fact that they're in Boise and we're 500 miles to the north. And I think the association in that respect represented the cities and their concerns to the legislature and proved to be a very necessary function. And I felt comfortable with that because through the association I was able to acquire and lead the city into the acquisition of several grants. One of them as an example was in the recreation area where we were able to obtain funding for some of the backstops and some of the fences around McEwen Field as a minor example. But the ability of the association to help the cities and even into smaller cities the size of Frenan And the rest I think was very fruitful to all of us because in effect we're all in this together. The pushing and shoving and the helping and assistance that we give to each other benefits the entire population. Don, I think sometimes that we often forget that things just don't happen overnight. I'm thinking now specifically as of last June 1st, I think it was, the revitalization of part of downtown Coeur d'Alene was completed. But a good number... First phase. First phase, excuse me. A number of years ago you were involved in a program that helped get this whole thing basically started. It was to get a grant to to do some studies. So what I'm getting at is, and you don't give the date here, but how many years ago were we involved in this? Well that was one of my first years on the city, or as mayor, and that would have been back in 78-79. We were concerned about the traffic flow in the, in, out of the downtown area and into the center core and in in essence the traffic flow throughout the city because there are numerous avenues even to this date that dead end and stop and go and and there are very few cross streets in the city. So I discovered through my, here again, my knowledge of people at the state level in the association that there was a grant available, and I was able to pick up the phone and talk to a member of the Economic Development Association, man to man, since I had met him through the association and had some very interesting talks and learned quite a bit from him, but he was able to help me acquire a $20, 000 grant to look at the traffic situation in and out of the Central Core and how we would address that. And that was the embryo that started all this. The first chairman of the of the Citizens Committee we formed on that was Bob Templin, who worked very hard and and worked toward the establishment of traffic flows in and out of the Central Core and eventually it evolved into the revitalization of the Downtown Core, but that was clear back over ten years ago. As you're sitting back now and looking out your window at downtown Coeur d'Alene, the first phase is completed. Did you envision it somewhat like it is? No, at one time we were very heavy into a covered mall. We thought that was attainable. As we moved and pushed and shoved, we found out—and there was three years of work put into that. It just couldn't come about. So this is the acceptable compromise that was arrived at. It was more economically available to the property owners and the merchants in the area and I'm real pleased with the way it came out. But it's been a lot of hard work by a lot of good people. I'm wondering, and I'm sure there are many people that I'm not even aware of who were involved. I'm wondering if Don Johnson had a little bit of influence on Sandy Bloom and Brad Jordan, who seemed to be in the newspaper a lot as far as revitalization. Well I don't want to step in here and take any credit because those two individuals as an example did an awful lot of work. I was fortunate enough to sit on most of the committees from 1978. And I hope that I contributed something, at least if nothing else, in the flow from one concept to the next. I'm sure you did. Okay, Don, now I think what we're going to do, we've done a little bit of your background, we've done some work on your councilman years, and now I'd like to talk to you a little bit about your tour of mayor which you were mayor of Coeur d'Alene from 1978 to 1982. You followed a good friend of yours, Rod Edinger, who was mayor, previous to that. So what I'd like to do is, I guess, number one, ask you, what do you feel in your mind was maybe the biggest contribution or maybe I could use the word accomplishment during your term as mayor with the guiding influence of your council, what do you think was the biggest contribution you folks made to the city of Coeur d'Alene at that time? Well I don't know that I would rate anything one, two, I think there are several things that happened. Unfortunately, the day I became mayor was the day the 1% initiative went into effect, and that put a curtailment on a lot of the ideas that the council and I had and it made it very difficult to try and put programs into effect but we worked very hard for four years on the enlargement of the sewer treatment plant, again an extension of what John McHugh had started earlier. We were under the gun again with the federal government. We worked very hard for four years and I was very disappointed that The program that we were working for was scrapped by the federal government in my last days in office. Four years of work did not happen, although they weren't totally scrapped, because it was the basis for the continuation of the improvements that were made at the treatment plant from 82 until the present. They were the groundwork for that. Some of the things that I was rather proud of was that the old junior high school on 7th and Montana was able to turn into a community center and I felt we were just months, a few months away from having the thing self-sufficient. Unfortunately those that followed didn't share that and we now have a park there instead of a community center. That was a little bit of a disappointment. I was pleased that, borrowing an idea from Governor Anderson's Idaho Tomorrow, I was able to, with the help of the college and Tony Stewart in particular, to initiate a Coeur d'Alene Tomorrow program where we brought in numerous members of the community and talked to the future of Coeur d'Alene that was very successful and I understand even today it is being drawn from in the planning and zoning area and I'm rather pleased about that. Can I interject one thing here? Don, during your term as mayor, the way things are, you know, it takes a long time getting certain things going. Was there, were you aware, was there talk at that time of a new Coeur d'Alene resort? Well, yes, prior to the Coeur d'Alene resort, if you recall, a gentleman by the name of Phillips was going to put together a multi-storied hotel, and a lot of the Seymour Shoreline committees developed during that period of time. And I think they were working with Mr. Templin at the time, and then I think Duane got involved, Duane Hagenau got involved, and you know, purchases and repurchase and everything made, and it evolved into the final concept of the resort. But yeah, we went through some difficult times there. But as I stated earlier, this is part of the process of getting public involved and things happening that are beneficial to all. Certainly we can all look back and appreciate the efforts and the heartaches that people went through, but the Coeur d'Alene Resort has to be a plus for the city. Yeah, absolutely. I don't see any problem with the resort. It's, you know, if we didn't have the resort today, I think in view of the closing of some of the mills and the mines up in the valley and the cutbacks at the Rolling Mill, I think this community would be in big trouble. So, you know, that's got to be a plus. And in that area, I think we have to recall that the seawall was built, brought to final, in its final form early in my terms as a councilman and when I got to Mary one of the first things we were able to accomplish was the enlargement of the of the boat ramps. I sometimes question whether that's a plus or not because all it did was create more pressure on the parking lot. Speaking of parking lot, Mayor, is there, in your opinion, a way to solve our summer weekend parking problem with boats? Yeah I think there is, but it's going to take some resolve on behalf of the community to recognize what the problem is and how to solve it, and that is that everybody can't have their own way. There's got to be compromise involved. I think one of the latest ideas that has been brought about that I think is an excellent idea is to let's dig down one story and put a lower level of parking in and retain the present level of parking on what would be the upper floor. The views would not be obstructed. I would think during the construction we could pick up more parking spaces Instead of say a 400 car parking lot we could have a parking lot approaching a thousand cars. That seemed to me like a viable idea that at least needs pursuing. That sounds like it'd be a great idea. But we got to bring some people together to do that. We talked a little bit about, and maybe you've already hit on, maybe one of your personally, not a personal disappointment to you during your term, but a stumbling block that was thrown in your way by the passing for that 1%. Excluding that, what would you say was probably the issue that you can recall during your tour of mayor that maybe caused you the most problems, not personally but as a mayor, in trying to get something solved? Well, there was a turmoil in the police department when I took took office. It required an awful lot of time. There was quite a bit of misunderstanding on quite a few people, and in particular I think the news media. They were looking for, I think, something to disappoint. I think it was a matter of some misunderstandings. It was resolved, let me cut that short by saying it was resolved when we were fortunate enough to find a gentleman by the name of Frank Primo, who was a professional police officer who had lots of experience in administration. We were able to bring him aboard and the problem just seemed to disappear overnight. It was, I think, a matter of organization that we needed to address and, you know, there again it was something like we were coming out of the small city into the emerging into a larger city and it's just one of those growing pains. That's what I was going to suggest. It seems to me, I think I remember who the police chief was at that time and it was kind of a... I know when I came... Excellent gentleman. Yeah. He's a good policeman. And you know, he took a lot of bad reps for that but it was resolved I think to the satisfaction of everybody. It's difficult times as those things happen but you know it was a real plus we came out on I think we ended up with an excellent Police Department. Question I want to ask you, and you can decline if you choose to not to answer this, but I was not here but you were obviously and during this time and How do you think that they ought to handle the issue of the hydroplanes returning to Coeur d'Alene Lake? Do you think there's a way to handle that that will solve everybody's... Well, I enjoyed the hydroplane races. A lot of fun. Unfortunately, they attracted an element that was not only offensive, but destructive to a lot of the community, and that create a lot of bad feelings. I guess the, and I hate to see us run into a vote every time this issue comes up, because this level will come up yearly, but there are such strong feelings about it, I think that it could be plugged into some area where we could have a vote, an advisory vote along with another election. You know, my idea there is not to cause added expense to the public, but an advisory vote seems like a legitimate way to go about this. In view of the statement you made just a few moments ago about one of the issues that caused you so many nights of staying up late, the police issue, and you felt that was solved and I'm sure it was solved by hiring a professional type law enforcement agent. Do you think that our police department now by bringing that type of individual in and has carried on up to this point that our law enforcement agency is sophisticated enough that we might not have those problems next summer as we might have had in the 60s? Well I think so. I think that you know the Dave Skates is chief now and I think that Frank trained him well. I think he's perfectly capable. There are other people I think also that could step into that on a moment's notice. I really feel that they have a professional organization over there now. Certainly you have problems from time to time, but I think the the police department is very well organized right now and operating fairly efficiently. You know, It has had its problem with the adoption of the Rat Patrol and all that. Yeah, right, not the Rat Patrol. I thought you were talking about down here on the boulevard. Well, the emphasis on speeders. I think in view of, and I watched the paper and I watched this area quite a bit, in the area of robberies, assaults, damage to property and that, I think is becoming a little bit excessive and I guess I'm troubled by the amount of time we spend on arresting speeders and don't misunderstand we certainly have to have traffic control. I think that I think the emphasis needs to be changed a little bit but I'm not that close to anymore and I shouldn't be sitting here critical but as a from what I hear on the street there's concern about the the number of robberies the number of house break-ins assaults and that nature. I don't think that's being critical. I think that view is shared by probably the bulk of the people of Coeur d'Alene. I think the pushing and shoving in that area will come, and I think that the demand will require more attention be paid to that area. I agree with that. Okay, Don, say most people that have been around Coeur d'Alene for a number of years know the name Don Johnston very well. He knows probably many things. A man that's not afraid to put on a pair of dirty coveralls, get out and work, paint, pour hot tar, get involved in the community, a real plus to the community and then when asked to do something that certainly volunteers. I think another way they remember you as as being a force and being very active in in city government not only as councilman but as a mayor. I guess I want to ask you a question. If I were to come up to you today, ten years from now, and say of all the years of service that you devoted to the city as a politician, what would you particularly like to be remembered for? I mean public service. It's something I enjoy. I've enjoyed government. There are other people that play tennis, play golf for a hobby. I like to think that government has been my hobby. There's a great amount of satisfaction when someone comes in with a problem, you're able to help them through it. You see them come back with a smile on his face and say, I didn't think we could pull this off, but we were able to get it together. If my staff was able to help him at the time and they were able to send that man away happy, there's a great deal of satisfaction to that. I've enjoyed that. Maybe it sounds corny, but I think a man was made to help fellow man. It's enjoyable if you look at it with that mind. I think far too few of us look for rewards like that, and I think far more of us should be looking for rewards like that. I think it's great. If more people would get involved, I would think, I like to think they would find out what fun it is. It's a lot of fun when you help somebody and everybody goes away happy. Okay Don, we're right coming down to the end of the the tape and I'm going to put you in a in a teacher role for just a short period of time. I've got myself involved in politics a little, I'm a hypothetical case now, I've got myself involved in politics a little bit and I'm coming to Don Johnson who has been through the scenario. I'm asking for advice. What should I do if I wish to become mayor? You have to have a commitment. You have to enjoy meeting and talking to people, but unless you're willing to make the commitment, spend the time, your family has to support you too. My wife is an example, has been supportive of anything I have endeavored to do. And believe me, that makes the job a lot easier. But it's got to be fun too. It's work, but if you don't like work, why don't you?

lines.line: # Introduction to Don's Community Involvement
> The interviewer establishes Don's extensive involvement in various community projects in Coeur d'Alene, highlighting his key role in the Park and Recreation Committee.
** Don, Coeur d'Alene, community, Park and Recreation **

lines.line: # Don's Early Memories and Contributions

> Don reflects on his initiation into Parks and Recreation from his grade school days. He discusses the informal beginnings of local sports organizing and his involvement in the development of structured recreational programs under the direction of Red Halpern.
** sports, Jim Egan, Red Halpern, Parks and Recreation **
keywords: ** sports, Jim Egan, Red Halpern, Parks and Recreation **

story.title: Johnston_Donald_OH1657_sideB
story.description: Interviewed by Denny Bozarth on April 12, 1990.
